risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "PACE was a parallel four-arm, multi-centre, randomised controlled trial" and "Allocation procedures have previously been described [5]." Comment: The paper explicitly describes the study as randomised, and although it does not restate the exact sequence-generation method in this article, the trial context and reference to previously described allocation procedures support that a proper random allocation process was used. Under a permissive standard, this is sufficient for Low risk.

Summary: The report states that this was a randomised trial and that "allocation procedures have previously been described [5]," but in the provided text there is no explicit description of how the next assignment was concealed from the recruiters/enrollers. Mention of randomisation or allocation procedures alone does not establish concealment. Comment: Because no central allocation, SNOSE, identical containers, or other concealment mechanism is explicitly described here, allocation concealment cannot be judged as low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "PACE was a parallel four-arm, multi-centre, randomised controlled trial..." and "SMC was provided by CFS doctors... APT involved management of energy expenditure... CBT, delivered by clinical psychologists or nurse therapists... GET, delivered by physiotherapists..." Comment: The report describes distinct behavioural/therapy interventions delivered by different personnel but does not indicate that participants or treating staff were blinded; given the obvious differences between CBT, GET, APT, and specialist medical care, knowledge of allocation would likely have been apparent. Because the main outcomes include self-reported fatigue, disability, and quality of life, such awareness could plausibly influence outcomes, so performance bias is high risk.

Summary: The main outcome measures were patient-reported questionnaires and self-reported service use/lost employment. The paper states that “QALYs were generated from the EQ-5D health-related quality of life questionnaire” and that “services used … were measured with the Client Service Receipt Inventory (CSRI)” and “Unpaid informal care … was measured by asking patients…”. It also reports fatigue and disability outcomes as proportions achieving clinically significant change on the Chalder fatigue questionnaire and SF-36 physical function sub-scale, both questionnaire-based outcomes. Comment: Because the patient is the assessor for these outcomes, lack of blinding of participants/outcome assessment could influence measurement; and since subjective/patient-reported outcomes are in the mix, the domain is High risk under the worst-outcome rule.

Quote: "Costs and QALYs were available for 570 (89%) participants (ranging from 85% GET to 93% SMC)." Comment: Attrition/missing outcome data for the analysed economic outcomes was moderate and fairly balanced across arms (about 7% to 15% loss per group; total loss about 11%), staying below the 20% per-arm threshold and without a ≥10 percentage point differential between arms. Although the authors note they "analysed data only for those participants where we had data at both baseline and follow-up," the amount and balance of missingness do not meet the hard high-risk thresholds, so this domain is judged low risk.
